ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for generic chocolate drink; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup (approximately 240 ml / 240 g) of chocolate drink, as typically prepared and consumed as a snack or light beverage.

nutrient_source.title: Chocolate drink, prepared from powder, with whole milk — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for chocolate drink (cr_id D04020002). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Chocolate-flavoured milk drink, GI value 34 (±4) — analogous estimate applied to generic chocolate drink
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI values of approximately 34–43 for chocolate-flavoured milk-based drinks. A GI of 40 (low category) is used as a defensible central estimate for this generic chocolate drink (10.47 g carbohydrate per 100 g). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (40 × 10.47 × 2.40) / 100 = 10.1 g per 240 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (7.58 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidance, despite low sodium (49 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this sugar-sweetened chocolate drink, as liquid added sugar independently raises blood pressure risk; choose an unsweetened cocoa or no-added-sugar variant instead.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 7.58 g sugar/100 g and zero dietary fibre; liquid sugar causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this chocolate drink because it is a sugar-sweetened beverage with 7.58 g sugar per 100 g and no fibre to slow absorption, causing rapid blood glucose spikes; an unsweetened cocoa powder preparation is a safer alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and elevated cardiovascular risk independent of total caloric intake.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit this sugar-sweetened chocolate drink due to its added sugar content (7.58 g/100 g), which is linked to dyslipidaemia and increased CVD risk; plain unsweetened cocoa in low-fat milk is a preferable option.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (7.58 g/100 g) with zero fibre and no meaningful anti-inflammatory components; potential pro-inflammatory palm-based creamer ingredients.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this chocolate drink due to its added sugar content and lack of anti-inflammatory nutrients; switching to unsweetened cocoa powder in water or milk preserves polyphenol benefit without the pro-inflammatory sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chocolate drink is a sweet ready-to-drink or powder-reconstituted beverage made by dissolving cocoa powder or chocolate syrup in milk or water, typically with added sugar. It is widely consumed as a snack or light refreshment across Singapore and globally. The drink has a mild chocolate flavour with a smooth, slightly sweet taste and thin to medium body depending on preparation. Per 100 g, it provides 64 kcal, 1.44 g protein, 1.86 g fat, and 10.47 g carbohydrate of which 7.58 g is sugar, with negligible dietary fibre (0 g) and moderate sodium (49 mg). The high proportion of sugar relative to total carbohydrate (72%) and the complete absence of dietary fibre classify this as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB). Clinically, SSBs are associated with rapid postprandial glucose elevation, increased triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and elevated blood pressure risk. This drink is not recommended for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 40 (low category) based on analogous chocolate milk drink data from the University of Sydney GI Database; however, the glycemic load per standard 240 g serving is approximately 10.1 g, which is clinically meaningful. Cocoa naturally contains flavanols with anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective properties, but the added sugar and processing in this formulation negate those benefits. An unsweetened cocoa powder preparation in low-fat milk is a nutritionally superior alternative.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in a chocolate drink?
Is cocoa drink good for blood pressure?
Is chocolate drink suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id D04020002) with confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for chocolate milk-based drinks, which is the closest approved GI source match.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source for this chocolate drink formulation. calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; these are confirmed zero values per the original HPB record and are retained as such. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, and all vitamin and mineral fields not reported by HPB are set to -1 (data not available in the HPB source for this entry). water_g set to -1: moisture data not reported in the HPB source for this entry. phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed micronutrient data not available in the HPB Singapore database for this chocolate drink entry as of 2026. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on chocolate-flavoured milk drink data from the University of Sydney GI Database (GI range 34–43 for similar products); this is not a direct measured value for this specific product. Glycemic load per 240 g serving = (40 × 10.47 × 2.40) / 100 = 10.1 g. The dietary_tags 'low_sodium' and 'low_calorie' from the input cultural section are noted; sodium at 49 mg/100 g is indeed low, and energy at 64 kcal/100 g is low per 100 g, but a standard 240 g serving delivers 154 kcal and 18.2 g sugar, which is clinically significant for SSB classification.
